main_paper.raw: {"methods":["CADO: Cost-Aware Diffusion solver for combinatorial Optimization (diffusion models with RL fine-tuning)","2-phase training framework: initial supervised learning followed by reinforcement learning fine-tuning","Integration of cost information and postprocess decoder into the training process"],"problems":["Combinatorial Optimization (CO) problems","Limitations of supervised learning-based heatmap solvers for CO (e.g., lack of cost-awareness, ignoring decoder impact, dependence on high-quality datasets)","Solving large-scale instances of CO problems such as TSP and MIS"],"datasets":["Synthetic TSP instances (node sizes 50 to 10,000)","Synthetic Maximum Independent Set (MIS) instances","TSPLIB real dataset benchmark"],"metrics":["Solution cost (e.g., tour length for TSP, size of independent set for MIS)","Performance compared to state-of-the-art methods"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance on TSP (node sizes 50 to 10,000) and MIS","value":"CADO outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods across all tested benchmarks"},{"metric":"Performance on TSPLIB real dataset benchmark","value":"CADO surpasses existing algorithms"}],"novelty_claims":["First to introduce RL fine-tuning for diffusion models in combinatorial optimization, directly incorporating cost information into training.","Addresses three key limitations of SL-based heatmap solvers: lack of cost-awareness, ignoring decoder impact, and dependence on high-quality datasets.","Demonstrates strong and consistent improvements over previous methods on both synthetic and real-world CO benchmarks.","Integrates the postprocess decoder into the training process, which is typically ignored in prior work."]}

methods: CADO: Cost-Aware Diffusion solver for combinatorial Optimization (diffusion models with RL fine-tuning)
2-phase training framework: initial supervised learning followed by reinforcement learning fine-tuning
Integration of cost information and postprocess decoder into the training process
problems: Combinatorial Optimization (CO) problems
Limitations of supervised learning-based heatmap solvers for CO (e.g., lack of cost-awareness, ignoring decoder impact, dependence on high-quality datasets)
Solving large-scale instances of CO problems such as TSP and MIS
metrics: Solution cost (e.g., tour length for TSP, size of independent set for MIS)

results.metric: Performance on TSP (node sizes 50 to 10,000) and MIS
results.value: CADO outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods across all tested benchmarks

results.metric: Performance on TSPLIB real dataset benchmark
results.value: CADO surpasses existing algorithms

novelty_claims: First to introduce RL fine-tuning for diffusion models in combinatorial optimization, directly incorporating cost information into training.
Addresses three key limitations of SL-based heatmap solvers: lack of cost-awareness, ignoring decoder impact, and dependence on high-quality datasets.
Demonstrates strong and consistent improvements over previous methods on both synthetic and real-world CO benchmarks.
Integrates the postprocess decoder into the training process, which is typically ignored in prior work.

methods: DIMES (Differentiable Meta Solver): a framework introducing a compact continuous space to parameterize the distribution of candidate solutions for combinatorial optimization problems
REINFORCE-based training and fine-tuning with massively parallel sampling
Meta-learning framework for effective initialization of model parameters across CO problem instances

Efficiently solving NP-hard CO problems such as the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) and Maximal Independent Set (MIS) on large graphs

novelty_claims: Introduction of a compact continuous space for parameterizing candidate solutions, enabling massively parallel sampling and efficient REINFORCE-based training for CO problems
First application of meta-learning over a collection of CO problem instances, treating each instance as a task for effective parameter initialization

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["DIFUSCO: a graph-based diffusion framework for combinatorial optimization","Formulation of NPC problems as {0,1}-vector optimization problems","Graph-based denoising diffusion models (with Gaussian and Bernoulli noise)","Message passing-based graph neural network for encoding and denoising","Anisotropic Graph Neural Network as backbone architecture","Effective inference schedule for solution quality enhancement"],"problems":["Solving NP-complete combinatorial optimization problems","Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)","Maximal Independent Set (MIS)"],"datasets":["TSP-500","TSP-1000","TSP-10000","SATLIB benchmark for MIS"],"metrics":["Performance gap to ground-truth (solution quality)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance gap to ground-truth on TSP-500","value":"0.46%"},{"metric":"Performance gap to ground-truth on TSP-1000","value":"1.17%"},{"metric":"Performance gap to ground-truth on TSP-10000","value":"2.58%"},{"metric":"Performance gap to ground-truth on TSP-500 (previous SOTA)","value":"1.76%"},{"metric":"Performance gap to ground-truth on TSP-1000 (previous SOTA)","value":"2.46%"},{"metric":"Performance gap to ground-truth on TSP-10000 (previous SOTA)","value":"3.19%"}],"novelty_claims":["First to use graph-based diffusion models for solving NP-complete combinatorial optimization problems.","Formulates NPC problems as {0,1}-vector optimization and applies denoising diffusion models directly to graph structures.","Investigates both continuous (Gaussian) and discrete (Bernoulli) diffusion models for these problems, showing discrete diffusion outperforms continuous.","Introduces an effective inference schedule for discrete diffusion solvers.","Demonstrates a single graph neural network backbone (Anisotropic GNN) can be used for different NPC problems (TSP and MIS).","Outperforms previous state-of-the-art neural solvers on standard benchmarks."]}

novelty_claims: First to use graph-based diffusion models for solving NP-complete combinatorial optimization problems.
Formulates NPC problems as {0,1}-vector optimization and applies denoising diffusion models directly to graph structures.
Investigates both continuous (Gaussian) and discrete (Bernoulli) diffusion models for these problems, showing discrete diffusion outperforms continuous.
Introduces an effective inference schedule for discrete diffusion solvers.
Demonstrates a single graph neural network backbone (Anisotropic GNN) can be used for different NPC problems (TSP and MIS).
Outperforms previous state-of-the-art neural solvers on standard benchmarks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Unified neural divide-and-conquer framework (UDC)","Divide-Conquer-Reunion (DCR) training method","High-efficiency Graph Neural Network (GNN) for instance dividing","Fixed-length sub-path constructive neural solver for conquering sub-problems"],"problems":["Large-scale combinatorial optimization (CO) problems","Generalization of neural combinatorial optimization to large-scale instances","Overcoming reliance on problem-specific heuristics in neural divide-and-conquer methods","Unified training of dividing and conquering policies in neural CO"],"datasets":["10 representative large-scale CO problems (specific datasets not named in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Solution quality (objective value)","Efficiency (runtime/performance speed)","Applicability/generalizability across CO problems"],"results":[{"metric":"Number of CO problems where UDC outperforms baselines","value":"10"},{"metric":"Applicability","value":"UDC applies to general CO problems without heuristic design"},{"metric":"Efficiency","value":"Significantly improved over existing large-scale NCO solvers (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)"},{"metric":"Solution quality","value":"Significantly outperforms existing large-scale NCO solvers (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a unified neural divide-and-conquer framework (UDC) for large-scale CO problems, unlike prior methods that rely on problem-specific heuristics.","Introduces the Divide-Conquer-Reunion (DCR) training method to jointly train dividing and conquering policies, considering their interdependencies and alleviating the negative impact of sub-optimal dividing policies.","UDC is the first to achieve a unified training scheme for neural divide-and-conquer methods, improving both solution quality and generalizability.","UDC demonstrates extensive applicability to a wide range of large-scale CO problems without relying on any heuristic design."]}

Significantly outperforms existing large-scale NCO solvers (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)

novelty_claims: Proposes a unified neural divide-and-conquer framework (UDC) for large-scale CO problems, unlike prior methods that rely on problem-specific heuristics.

Introduces the Divide-Conquer-Reunion (DCR) training method to jointly train dividing and conquering policies, considering their interdependencies and alleviating the negative impact of sub-optimal dividing policies.
UDC is the first to achieve a unified training scheme for neural divide-and-conquer methods, improving both solution quality and generalizability.
UDC demonstrates extensive applicability to a wide range of large-scale CO problems without relying on any heuristic design.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Generic framework to cast any Combinatorial Optimization Problem (COP) as a Markov Decision Process (MDP)","Bisimulation Quotienting (BQ) to reduce the state space of MDPs for COPs","Explicit bisimulation mapping for recursive COPs","Transformer-based (attention-based) policy network for BQ-MDPs","Imitation learning from (near) optimal solutions of small instances","Beam search for improved performance","Linear attention-based model (PerceiverIO) for faster execution"],"problems":["Out-of-distribution generalization in neural combinatorial optimization","Efficient and generalizable neural solvers for combinatorial optimization problems","Exploiting symmetries in COPs for improved learning and efficiency"],"datasets":["Synthetic benchmarks for TSP, ATSP, CVRP, OP, KP","Realistic benchmarks for TSP, ATSP, CVRP, OP, KP"],"metrics":["Solution quality (e.g., tour length, total value)","Generalization to larger instances","Computation time/execution speed"],"results":[{"metric":"State-of-the-art performance on five COPs (TSP, ATSP, CVRP, OP, KP)","value":"Outperforms previous neural-based constructive methods, especially for instances with more than 200 nodes"},{"metric":"Generalization to larger instances","value":"Excellent; learned policies generalize to much larger instances than seen during training"},{"metric":"Execution speed (PerceiverIO vs Transformer)","value":"Significantly faster with PerceiverIO at slight performance cost"}],"novelty_claims":["First generic and principled framework to derive a direct MDP for any COP with minimal requirements and prove equivalence between optimal MDP policies and COP solutions","Introduction of bisimulation quotienting to reduce MDP state space for COPs, exploiting symmetries not limited to Euclidean graphs but to recursive properties common in COPs","Explicit bisimulation mapping for recursive COPs, enabling significant state space reduction and efficient learning","Design of a transformer-based architecture for BQ-MDPs that works across multiple COPs with minor adaptations","Achieves new state-of-the-art generalization and performance on five classical COPs, especially for large out-of-distribution instances"]}

novelty_claims: First generic and principled framework to derive a direct MDP for any COP with minimal requirements and prove equivalence between optimal MDP policies and COP solutions
Introduction of bisimulation quotienting to reduce MDP state space for COPs, exploiting symmetries not limited to Euclidean graphs but to recursive properties common in COPs
Explicit bisimulation mapping for recursive COPs, enabling significant state space reduction and efficient learning
Design of a transformer-based architecture for BQ-MDPs that works across multiple COPs with minor adaptations
Achieves new state-of-the-art generalization and performance on five classical COPs, especially for large out-of-distribution instances

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Sym-NCO: a regularizer-based training scheme leveraging symmetricities (rotational and reflectional invariance) in combinatorial optimization problems","Use of REINFORCE algorithm with specially designed baseline terms to impart solution and problem symmetries"],"problems":["Improving the generalization and performance of deep reinforcement learning-based neural combinatorial optimization (DRL-NCO) methods","Closing the performance gap between DRL-NCO and state-of-the-art conventional heuristics for combinatorial optimization problems"],"datasets":["Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) instances","Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) instances","Prize Collecting TSP (PCTSP) instances","Orienteering Problem (OP) instances"],"metrics":["Solution quality (e.g., tour length, cost)","Computation speed (inference time)"],"results":[{"metric":"PCTSP performance vs. ILS","value":"Sym-NCO outperformed ILS at 240x faster speed"},{"metric":"Performance improvement over existing DRL-NCO methods","value":"Sym-NCO greatly improves performance in TSP, CVRP, PCTSP, and OP (exact numbers not specified in provided text)"}],"novelty_claims":["Sym-NCO is a general regularization-based training scheme that leverages universal symmetricities in combinatorial optimization problems, rather than requiring problem-specific expert domain knowledge.","Sym-NCO can improve existing powerful CO models without redesigning neural network architectures, unlike equivariant neural network schemes.","Sym-NCO imparts symmetricity at the training scheme level, making it broadly applicable to various CO problems."]}

methods: Sym-NCO: a regularizer-based training scheme leveraging symmetricities (rotational and reflectional invariance) in combinatorial optimization problems
Use of REINFORCE algorithm with specially designed baseline terms to impart solution and problem symmetries
problems: Improving the generalization and performance of deep reinforcement learning-based neural combinatorial optimization (DRL-NCO) methods
Closing the performance gap between DRL-NCO and state-of-the-art conventional heuristics for combinatorial optimization problems

results.value: Sym-NCO outperformed ILS at 240x faster speed

results.metric: Performance improvement over existing DRL-NCO methods
results.value: Sym-NCO greatly improves performance in TSP, CVRP, PCTSP, and OP (exact numbers not specified in provided text)

novelty_claims: Sym-NCO is a general regularization-based training scheme that leverages universal symmetricities in combinatorial optimization problems, rather than requiring problem-specific expert domain knowledge.
Sym-NCO can improve existing powerful CO models without redesigning neural network architectures, unlike equivariant neural network schemes.
Sym-NCO imparts symmetricity at the training scheme level, making it broadly applicable to various CO problems.

Introduces a three-stage reinforcement learning-based training scheme that does not require labeled optimal solutions and enables cross-scale feature learning.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Light Encoder and Heavy Decoder (LEHD) model","Data-efficient training scheme: learn to construct partial solution","Flexible solution construction mechanism: Random Re-Construct (RRC)"],"problems":["Generalization of neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) methods to large-scale combinatorial optimization problems","Solving large-scale instances of the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) and Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP)","Overcoming poor generalization of existing NCO models to large-scale problems"],"datasets":["Synthetic TSP instances (up to 1000 nodes)","Synthetic CVRP instances (up to 1000 nodes)","TSPLib","CVRPLib"],"metrics":["Solution quality (e.g., tour length for TSP, total cost for CVRP)","Generalization performance to larger problem sizes"],"results":[{"metric":"Solution quality on TSP and CVRP (up to 1000 nodes)","value":"Nearly optimal solutions, state-of-the-art performance"},{"metric":"Generalization to real-world TSPLib/CVRPLib","value":"Model generalizes well, achieving robust and promising performance"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel Light Encoder and Heavy Decoder (LEHD) model structure for NCO, which dynamically captures relationships among nodes and enables scale-independent feature learning.","Introduces a data-efficient supervised training scheme (learn to construct partial solution) for robust model training.","Develops a flexible solution construction mechanism (Random Re-Construct) for active improvement at inference.","Demonstrates that the LEHD model, trained only on small-scale instances, generalizes to large-scale problems (up to 1000 nodes) and real-world datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results."]}

methods: Light Encoder and Heavy Decoder (LEHD) model
Data-efficient training scheme: learn to construct partial solution
Flexible solution construction mechanism: Random Re-Construct (RRC)
problems: Generalization of neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) methods to large-scale combinatorial optimization problems

metrics: Solution quality (e.g., tour length for TSP, total cost for CVRP)
Generalization performance to larger problem sizes

results.metric: Solution quality on TSP and CVRP (up to 1000 nodes)
results.value: Nearly optimal solutions, state-of-the-art performance
Model generalizes well, achieving robust and promising performance

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel Light Encoder and Heavy Decoder (LEHD) model structure for NCO, which dynamically captures relationships among nodes and enables scale-independent feature learning.
Introduces a data-efficient supervised training scheme (learn to construct partial solution) for robust model training.
Develops a flexible solution construction mechanism (Random Re-Construct) for active improvement at inference.
Demonstrates that the LEHD model, trained only on small-scale instances, generalizes to large-scale problems (up to 1000 nodes) and real-world datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Policy Optimization with Multiple Optima (POMO)","Modified REINFORCE algorithm with multiple diverse rollouts","Low-variance baseline for policy gradient","Augmentation-based inference method","Multiple greedy rollouts for inference"],"problems":["Neural combinatorial optimization for NP-hard problems","Efficient and generalizable reinforcement learning for combinatorial optimization","Reducing optimality gap and inference time in combinatorial optimization solvers","Avoiding local minima in RL-based combinatorial optimization"],"datasets":["Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) instances (e.g., TSP100)","Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) instances","0-1 Knapsack Problem (KP) instances"],"metrics":["Optimality gap","Inference time"],"results":[{"metric":"Optimality gap (TSP100)","value":"0.14%"},{"metric":"Inference time reduction","value":"More than an order of magnitude faster than previous methods"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of POMO, a policy optimization method that exploits symmetries in combinatorial optimization problems via multiple diverse rollouts.","A new low-variance baseline for policy gradient derived from heterogeneous trajectories, improving training stability and resistance to local minima.","A new augmentation-based inference method that leverages problem symmetries for improved performance.","Demonstration that POMO achieves superior performance (lower optimality gap and faster inference) compared to all recent learned heuristics on TSP, CVRP, and KP, using the same neural net and training method without problem-specific heuristics."]}

methods: Policy Optimization with Multiple Optima (POMO)
Modified REINFORCE algorithm with multiple diverse rollouts
Low-variance baseline for policy gradient
Augmentation-based inference method
Multiple greedy rollouts for inference
problems: Neural combinatorial optimization for NP-hard problems
Efficient and generalizable reinforcement learning for combinatorial optimization
Reducing optimality gap and inference time in combinatorial optimization solvers
Avoiding local minima in RL-based combinatorial optimization

results.value: More than an order of magnitude faster than previous methods

novelty_claims: Introduction of POMO, a policy optimization method that exploits symmetries in combinatorial optimization problems via multiple diverse rollouts.
A new low-variance baseline for policy gradient derived from heterogeneous trajectories, improving training stability and resistance to local minima.
A new augmentation-based inference method that leverages problem symmetries for improved performance.

Demonstration that POMO achieves superior performance (lower optimality gap and faster inference) compared to all recent learned heuristics on TSP, CVRP, and KP, using the same neural net and training method without problem-specific heuristics.

methods: Learning What to Defer (LwD): a novel deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework that adaptively determines which elements to decide or defer at each stage for combinatorial optimization problems
Diversification bonus: a novel reward mechanism to encourage the generation of diverse candidate solutions by coupling MDPs and rewarding deviation between solutions
problems: Scalability of DRL-based solvers for large-scale combinatorial optimization problems, specifically the maximum independent set (MIS) problem
Inefficiency of existing DRL solvers that require a number of stages proportional to the solution size, limiting applicability to large graphs
Generalization of learning-based solvers to unseen graph types and other locally decomposable combinatorial optimization problems
metrics: Objective value (size of the independent set found)

results.metric: Speedup over KaMIS on Barabasi-Albert graph (2 million vertices)
results.value: LwD achieves better objectives 13 times faster (3637/276) compared to KaMIS
LwD consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art DRL method (Khalil et al., 2017) and often outperforms KaMIS, especially on large-scale graphs

novelty_claims: Proposes a new DRL framework (LwD) that adaptively stretches or shrinks the number of decision stages by learning to defer element-wise decisions, enabling efficient solution of large-scale combinatorial optimization problems
Introduces a diversification bonus in the reward function to explicitly encourage the generation of diverse candidate solutions, improving performance under time constraints

Demonstrates that LwD can outperform both state-of-the-art DRL and conventional MIS solvers (e.g., KaMIS) on large-scale graphs, and generalizes to unseen graph types and other locally decomposable combinatorial optimization problems

methods: DeepACO: a neural-enhanced Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) meta-heuristic leveraging deep reinforcement learning to automate heuristic design
Three extended implementations of DeepACO: (1) multihead decoder, (2) additional top-k entropy loss, (3) additional imitation loss, for better exploration-exploitation balance
problems: Automating heuristic design in Ant Colony Optimization for Combinatorial Optimization Problems (COPs)
Reducing reliance on expert knowledge and manual tuning in ACO
Improving generalization and performance of ACO across diverse COPs

results.value: DeepACO consistently outperforms ACO counterparts on eight COPs using a single neural model and hyperparameters
DeepACO performs better than or on par with problem-specific NCO methods on canonical routing problems

novelty_claims: First to exploit deep reinforcement learning to guide the evolution of ACO meta-heuristics
DeepACO automates heuristic design, removing the need for manual, expert-driven heuristics in ACO
DeepACO is the most broadly evaluated Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) technique, demonstrating effectiveness across eight diverse COPs with a single neural model and hyperparameters
Proposed three generalizable extensions (multihead decoder, top-k entropy loss, imitation loss) for balancing exploration and exploitation in heatmap-based NCO methods

methods: BRAID: Doubly Conservative Fine-Tuning of Diffusion Models (conservative reward modeling + KL penalization)
Training a conservative reward model with uncertainty quantification to penalize out-of-distribution regions
Fine-tuning pre-trained diffusion models using the conservative reward model and KL penalty
problems: Overoptimization in offline model-based optimization due to reward model extrapolation in out-of-distribution regions
Combining generative modeling and model-based optimization for design tasks in offline settings where reward models are unknown and only static datasets are available
Preventing generation of invalid designs when optimizing for high-reward designs using generative models
novelty_claims: Introduction of BRAID, a doubly conservative fine-tuning framework for diffusion models that combines conservative reward modeling and KL penalization to prevent overoptimization in offline design tasks.
First to address the problem of overoptimization in offline RL-based fine-tuning of diffusion models for scientific design tasks where reward models are unknown and only static data is available.
Theoretical justification for the use of conservative terms in the fine-tuning process, showing improved regret and the ability to outperform the best designs in the offline data.
Empirical demonstration of the approach's effectiveness across diverse domains, including DNA/RNA sequence and image design.

methods: Moco: a learnable meta-optimizer using a graph neural network (GNN) to update solution construction procedures for combinatorial optimization problems
Meta-training procedure targeting the best solution found during the search, conditioned on features such as search budget and current search state
GNN-based approach for updating the heatmap parameter vector (theta) during inference, replacing Adam optimizer used in prior work (Dimes)
Learning to Optimize (L2O) framework with evolutionary strategies to train the meta-optimizer
problems: Learning general heuristics for combinatorial optimization problems (COPs), specifically NP-hard problems
Improving solution quality and adaptability of neural combinatorial optimization methods, especially for varying computational budgets
Overcoming limitations of direct solution construction and simple update mechanisms in existing neural combinatorial optimization approaches
metrics: Performance relative to other approaches (e.g., outperformance, competitiveness)

results.value: Moco outperforms other approaches
Moco is overall competitive, especially outperforming related approaches, even those using additional local search

novelty_claims: Moco is a fully learnable meta-optimizer for combinatorial optimization that does not rely on problem-specific local search or decomposition.
Moco uses a novel GNN-based meta-optimizer to update the solution construction process based on the current search state and budget, improving over previous meta-optimizer architectures.
Moco adapts its optimization strategy to different inference budgets, including those not seen during training, and learns budget-dependent strategies.
Moco significantly improves over established heatmap-based approaches and is competitive with or outperforms other state-of-the-art methods.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SoftDist (a simple baseline method applying softmax to the TSP distance matrix for heatmap generation)","Critical evaluation of ML-based heatmap generation methods for TSP","Introduction of the Score metric for fair comparison between MCTS and LKH-3"],"problems":["Effectiveness of ML-based heatmap generation for guiding MCTS in large-scale TSPs","Practical value and efficiency of the heatmap-guided MCTS paradigm compared to traditional heuristics like LKH-3","Inconsistencies between training and testing phases in ML-based heatmap generation for TSP"],"datasets":["Large-scale TSP instances (specific datasets not named, but large-scale synthetic or benchmark TSP instances are implied)"],"metrics":["Solution Quality","Inference Latency","Score (relative performance metric comparing MCTS to LKH-3)"],"results":[{"metric":"Solution Quality (SoftDist vs. ML-based methods)","value":"SoftDist outperforms most complex ML-based heatmap generation methods and achieves comparable performance to the fully-supervised DIFUSCO method."},{"metric":"Inference Latency (SoftDist vs. ML-based methods)","value":"SoftDist achieves faster inference (heatmap generation) than complex ML-based methods."},{"metric":"Score (MCTS vs. LKH-3)","value":"Heatmap-guided MCTS methods significantly underperform compared to LKH-3, regardless of runtime or parameter tuning."}],"novelty_claims":["First comprehensive critique of ML-based heatmap generation and the heatmap-guided MCTS paradigm for TSPs.","Introduction of SoftDist, a simple and effective heatmap generation method that outperforms complex ML-based approaches.","Proposal of the Score metric for fair and direct comparison between heatmap-guided MCTS and LKH-3 under identical hardware and time constraints."]}

methods: SoftDist (a simple baseline method applying softmax to the TSP distance matrix for heatmap generation)
Critical evaluation of ML-based heatmap generation methods for TSP
Introduction of the Score metric for fair comparison between MCTS and LKH-3
problems: Effectiveness of ML-based heatmap generation for guiding MCTS in large-scale TSPs
Practical value and efficiency of the heatmap-guided MCTS paradigm compared to traditional heuristics like LKH-3
Inconsistencies between training and testing phases in ML-based heatmap generation for TSP
datasets: Large-scale TSP instances (specific datasets not named, but large-scale synthetic or benchmark TSP instances are implied)
metrics: Score (relative performance metric comparing MCTS to LKH-3)

results.value: SoftDist outperforms most complex ML-based heatmap generation methods and achieves comparable performance to the fully-supervised DIFUSCO method.
SoftDist achieves faster inference (heatmap generation) than complex ML-based methods.
Heatmap-guided MCTS methods significantly underperform compared to LKH-3, regardless of runtime or parameter tuning.

novelty_claims: First comprehensive critique of ML-based heatmap generation and the heatmap-guided MCTS paradigm for TSPs.
Introduction of SoftDist, a simple and effective heatmap generation method that outperforms complex ML-based approaches.
Proposal of the Score metric for fair and direct comparison between heatmap-guided MCTS and LKH-3 under identical hardware and time constraints.

methods: Deep reinforcement learning algorithm trained via policy gradient to learn 2-opt improvement heuristics
Policy neural network with pointer attention mechanism for action selection
Stochastic policy learning for selecting 2-opt operations
Incorporation of search history by tracking current best-visited solution
problems: Learning improvement heuristics for the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)

results.value: Learned policies achieve near-optimal solutions even from poor initial solutions

novelty_claims: First to propose learning a local search heuristic for TSP based on 2-opt operators via deep reinforcement learning
